text.title: azure-compute Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for azure-compute. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Choose VMs only for legacy applications requiring specific OS versions, custom kernel modules, or licensing tied to VM instances.
rule.rationale: VMs provide full OS control but require the most operational overhead (patching, scaling, HA configuration). Every other scenario maps to a higher abstraction tier with less burden.

rule.statement: Use VMSS Flexible orchestration (not Uniform) for all new scale-out workloads; Uniform mode is being deprecated.
rule.rationale: Flexible orchestration supports standard VM APIs, mixed instance types, and max zone-spreading. Uniform mode uses a separate scale set VM API surface that Microsoft is retiring.

rule.statement: Choose AKS for containerized workloads that require Kubernetes features: custom operators, advanced scheduling, multi-tenancy, or complex networking.
rule.rationale: AKS gives full Kubernetes API access with a free managed control plane (99.95% SLA). Teams without Kubernetes expertise should evaluate Container Apps first — AKS operational overhead is significant.

rule.statement: Use Container Apps for event-driven microservices that benefit from scale-to-zero; use AKS when you need control plane access, custom operators, or ARM64 nodes.
rule.rationale: Container Apps is built on Kubernetes (Dapr + KEDA + Envoy) but hides all Kubernetes complexity. It does not support Windows containers or ARM64 as of 2026. AKS supports both.

rule.statement: Use App Service for web applications and APIs with predictable traffic where slot-based blue-green deployments are sufficient; prefer Container Apps for event-driven or variable-load services.
rule.rationale: App Service deployment slots enable zero-downtime swaps with auto-swap for CD pipelines. Container Apps is better for bursty workloads because it can scale to zero, eliminating idle compute cost.

rule.statement: Select B-series VMs for workloads with variable CPU usage (CI agents, dev environments, low-traffic web servers); use D-series for balanced production workloads.
rule.rationale: B-series accumulates CPU credits during idle periods and uses them during bursts — ideal when average utilization is below 20%. D-series provides consistent CPU without burst limits, suitable for production APIs.

rule.statement: Enable Availability Zones for all production VMs and VMSS (99.99% SLA); Availability Sets provide only 99.95% SLA and do not protect against zone failures.
rule.rationale: Availability Zones place VMs in physically separate datacenters within a region. A zone failure (rare but documented) takes down all VMs in that zone. Zone-redundant deployments survive such events.

rule.statement: Use Standard tier or higher for production App Service plans; Free and Basic tiers lack autoscale, custom domains with TLS, and deployment slots.
rule.rationale: Standard is the minimum viable tier for production: it includes 5 deployment slots, VNet integration, autoscale, and custom domain TLS. Premium adds more scale-out capacity and improved performance.

rule.statement: Use Workload Identity (OIDC federation) for pod-level Azure resource access — never use the deprecated AAD Pod Identity or store service principal credentials in Secrets.
rule.rationale: Workload Identity uses Kubernetes ServiceAccount token projection with Azure AD OIDC federation. It requires no secret management: the cluster issues a signed JWT that Azure AD verifies directly. Pod Identity is deprecated and will be removed.

rule.statement: Enable the OIDC issuer and Workload Identity add-on before enabling KEDA — KEDA's TriggerAuthentication uses Workload Identity to access Azure queues, Service Bus, and Event Hub without credentials.
rule.rationale: KEDA's Azure scalers authenticate to Azure via Workload Identity when the cluster has OIDC issuer enabled. Installing KEDA before OIDC/Workload Identity leaves scalers unable to authenticate to Azure backends.

rule.statement: Create a system node pool with small VMs (D2s_v5), taints to prevent user workloads, and a dedicated user node pool with autoscaling across all three availability zones.
rule.rationale: System node pools run CoreDNS and kube-proxy. Mixing user workloads onto system nodes risks evicting critical cluster components under memory pressure. Separation ensures stable cluster operations regardless of workload load spikes.

rule.statement: Add a Spot node pool (priority=Spot, min=0, max=N) for batch and interruptible workloads to reduce compute costs by up to 90%; add tolerations to workloads that run on Spot nodes.
rule.rationale: Spot VMs are evicted with 30 seconds notice. Applications that handle preemption gracefully (batch jobs, stateless workers) run correctly on Spot. Applications requiring persistent connections (databases, session-sensitive APIs) must never run on Spot pools.

rule.statement: Set max_surge to 33% for all node pool upgrade settings to control rolling upgrade speed while maintaining capacity during upgrades.
rule.rationale: 33% surge allows one-third of nodes to be upgraded at a time. Lower values slow upgrades unnecessarily; higher values risk under-capacity if surge nodes are slow to provision.

rule.statement: Use Azure CNI Overlay networking for large clusters (hundreds of nodes) to avoid exhausting subnet IP space; use standard Azure CNI for clusters where pod IPs must be directly routable in the VNet.
rule.rationale: Standard Azure CNI assigns a VNet IP to every pod, consuming IPs rapidly at scale. Overlay mode assigns pod IPs from a private CIDR that is NAT'd at the node level, decoupling pod count from VNet IP consumption.

rule.statement: Enable automatic_channel_upgrade: patch with a maintenance window on off-peak hours to receive automatic patch version upgrades without manual intervention.
rule.rationale: Unpatched Kubernetes clusters accumulate CVEs. Automatic patch upgrades within the maintenance window keep clusters current without disrupting business hours. Minor version upgrades still require manual approval for testing.

rule.statement: Set min_replicas: 0 for batch and background workloads to enable scale-to-zero; set min_replicas: 1 or higher for latency-sensitive APIs to avoid cold-start latency on first request.
rule.rationale: Scale-to-zero eliminates idle compute cost. However, a cold start from zero replicas adds 1–5 seconds latency depending on image size. APIs with SLA requirements on first-request latency must maintain at least one warm replica.

rule.statement: Configure KEDA scale rules for event-driven sources (Azure Service Bus, Storage Queue, Event Hub) to scale containers based on queue depth rather than CPU/memory metrics.
rule.rationale: CPU/memory-based scaling reacts to load after it has already arrived. Queue-depth scaling is proactive: containers are provisioned as messages accumulate, before processing falls behind.

rule.statement: Use revision_mode: Multiple with named revision labels to implement blue-green or canary deployments via traffic weight splitting — never delete the old revision before verifying the new one is healthy.
rule.rationale: Traffic splitting in Multiple mode allows incremental rollout (e.g., 10% → 50% → 100%) and instant rollback by shifting 100% traffic back to the previous revision. Single revision mode requires a full cutover with no gradual validation.

rule.statement: Enable Dapr sidecar for service-to-service communication and state management in microservices — use Dapr component scopes to limit which containers can access each component.
rule.rationale: Dapr component scopes enforce least-privilege: a payment service can access the payments state store but not the orders state store, even though both run in the same environment. Without scopes, any app can read/write any Dapr component.

rule.statement: Use Spot Priority Mix on VMSS to maintain a minimum on-demand baseline while running the remainder as Spot VMs; calculate the split using baseRegularPriorityCount + regularPriorityPercentageAboveBase.
rule.rationale: Pure Spot is unreliable for production — eviction rates can exceed 20% during peak Azure demand. Spot Priority Mix guarantees a minimum on-demand baseline (e.g., 2 VMs) while saving up to 90% on the remaining capacity, making it safe for production stateless workloads.

rule.statement: Purchase Reserved Instances for stable baseline workloads (VMs, AKS system nodes, App Service plans) with 1-year commitments; use Savings Plans for flexible cross-service commitments.
rule.rationale: Reserved Instances provide up to 72% savings over pay-as-you-go for predictable workloads. Savings Plans offer similar savings but apply across compute services, useful when the mix of VMs vs containers changes over time.

rule.statement: Use Dev/Test pricing for all non-production environments and scale down or stop non-prod resources outside business hours.
rule.rationale: Dev/Test pricing removes the Windows Server license fee from eligible VMs and App Service plans, reducing cost by up to 55%. Stopping non-prod VMs overnight (16h/day off) reduces compute cost by approximately 67% compared to always-on.

rule.statement: Use Managed Identity for all Azure resource access from VMs, AKS, Container Apps, and App Service — never store service principal credentials in code, environment variables, or Kubernetes Secrets.
rule.rationale: Managed Identities are issued and rotated by Azure AD automatically. A stored service principal credential is a permanent secret that can be exfiltrated; a Managed Identity credential expires with the VM/pod and cannot be used outside its assigned resource.

rule.statement: Configure private endpoints for all PaaS services accessed by compute resources (Azure SQL, Storage, Key Vault, Container Registry) to route traffic over the Azure backbone instead of the public internet.
rule.rationale: Private endpoints bind a PaaS service to a private VNet IP. Traffic stays on Microsoft's backbone and is not exposed to internet routing, DDoS, or man-in-the-middle risks. NSG rules can then block all outbound internet from compute nodes.

rule.statement: Enable Microsoft Defender for Containers (AKS) and Defender for VMs for threat detection; enforce Azure Policy assignments to prevent non-compliant resource configurations from being deployed.
rule.rationale: Defender for Containers scans running images, detects privilege escalation attempts, and alerts on anomalous network activity. Azure Policy prevents teams from deploying resources without required tags, encryption, or network restrictions, shifting security left before deployment.
